cases.title: Pinpoint Pupils in an Alley
cases.presentation: A 29-year-old man is found unresponsive in an alley with a respiratory rate of 4/min, pinpoint pupils, and drug paraphernalia nearby.
cases.question: What toxidrome does this match, and what is the field priority?
options: A: Anticholinergic toxidrome — administer IV fluids as the priority.
B: Opioid toxidrome — prioritize airway/ventilation support immediately, since this is fundamentally a respiratory emergency.
C: Cholinergic toxidrome — administer atropine as the sole intervention.
D: Sympathomimetic toxidrome — sedate the patient.
cases.explanation: Pinpoint pupils, severe respiratory depression, and altered mental status match the opioid toxidrome. This is fundamentally a respiratory emergency, so airway/ventilation support is the immediate priority, with naloxone as an adjunct per local protocol.
cases.clinicalPearl: Recognize the pattern and act — pinpoint pupils plus slow breathing means ventilate first, identify the exact substance later.

cases.title: Confused and Sweating on the Trail
cases.presentation: A 34-year-old hiker is found confused and stumbling on a hot afternoon. She is still visibly sweating, and her core temperature reads 105.2°F (40.7°C).
cases.question: Does the presence of sweating change the diagnosis?
options: A: Yes, sweating rules out heat stroke and this should be treated as heat exhaustion.
B: No — altered mental status combined with a significantly elevated core temperature still meets the definition of heat stroke, regardless of sweating. Begin aggressive cooling and rapid transport.
C: Sweating means this is not a heat-related illness at all.
D: This finding is irrelevant to field management.
cases.explanation: The presence of sweating does not rule out heat stroke — some heat stroke patients, especially with exertional causes, continue to sweat. The defining combination is altered mental status plus significantly elevated core temperature, both present here. This is heat stroke, a true emergency requiring aggressive cooling and rapid transport.
cases.clinicalPearl: Don't let sweating fool you — confused plus very hot is heat stroke, sweaty or not.

cases.title: Found Cold and Unresponsive
cases.presentation: A 58-year-old man is found unresponsive outdoors after an unknown period of cold exposure. He has no palpable pulse and no visible respirations. His core temperature reads 79°F (26°C).
cases.question: What is the correct field approach?
options: A: Withhold resuscitation since the patient is severely hypothermic and appears dead.
B: Begin resuscitation per protocol, handling the patient gently — severe hypothermia can mimic death, and patients have survived with good outcomes after rewarming.
C: Immediately declare death without further assessment.
D: Move the patient rapidly and vigorously to the ambulance to begin transport as fast as possible.
cases.explanation: Severe hypothermia can mimic death, and patients have survived prolonged downtime with good neurologic outcomes after rewarming — resuscitation should generally proceed per local protocol rather than being presumed futile. The patient should also be handled gently throughout, since rough movement can trigger a fatal arrhythmia in a severely hypothermic heart.
cases.clinicalPearl: Not dead until warm and dead — resuscitate a hypothermic arrest patient per protocol, and handle them gently while you do.
